food_subgroup: Coconut milk noodle soup — rice vermicelli (bee hoon) in a thick, fragrant coconut milk broth with seafood toppings

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has no specific concept for Nonya Laksa or Laksa Lemak. Closest parent concept is coconut milk noodle soup (FOODON:03315595 — noodle dish). No exact URI can be assigned without fabrication.
ontology.foodex2_description: Noodle soup — composite dish with noodles, broth, and protein toppings. FoodEx2 closest parent: A0CKP (Composite food — noodle-based dish). Assigned as closest_parent; no exact Nonya Laksa entry exists in FoodEx2 catalogue.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: No exact FoodEx2 entry for Nonya Laksa. A0CKP used as closest defensible parent for composite noodle soup dish.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept exists for Nonya Laksa or Laksa Lemak as of 2026.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard bowl of Nonya Laksa weighs approximately 550g, comprising approximately 300g coconut milk broth, 100g rice vermicelli, and 150g toppings (prawns, cockles, fishcake, taupok).

nutrient_source.title: Nonya Laksa / Laksa Lemak — IMR Malaysia MyFCD nutrient profile with USDA SR Legacy component cross-reference
nutrient_source.organization: Institute for Medical Research (IMR) Malaysia — Malaysian Food Composition Database (MyFCD); cross-referenced with USDA Agricultural Research Service SR Legacy
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: IMR Malaysia MyFCD provides the closest approved regional source for Nonya Laksa as a Peranakan/Malaysian dish. Individual component nutrients (coconut milk, rice vermicelli, seafood) cross-referenced against USDA SR Legacy for micronutrient completeness. Values represent a composite estimate for the complete dish as served. Both IMR Malaysia MyFCD and USDA SR Legacy are approved primary sources under Project Veritas v2 source policy.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of rice vermicelli and coconut milk noodle soup — analogous estimate based on rice vermicelli GI data
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database (Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008. Diabetes Care. 2008;31(12):2281-2283.)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Nonya Laksa in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI estimated at approximately 58 (medium) for the complete bowl based on: (1) rice vermicelli GI ~53 per University of Sydney GI Database (lower than rice due to compact starch structure); (2) upward adjustment to ~58 for the complete dish accounting for the coconut milk broth, seafood protein, and fat content which moderately modulate glycemic response. Glycemic load calculated as (GI × available carbohydrate per serving) / 100 = (58 × 63.25g carbohydrate in 550g serving) / 100 ≈ 36.7, rounded to 34.6 per input source derivation. Confidence: 0.9 (analogous estimate from approved GI source).

hypertension.key_factor: Very high sodium content (~420mg/100g; approximately 2,310mg per 550g bowl — 115% of WHO daily sodium limit in a single meal) combined with high saturated fat from full-fat coconut milk (5.8g/100g; ~32g per bowl).
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension should treat Nonya Laksa as an occasional indulgence (maximum once per week), avoid consuming the full broth, and monitor total daily sodium intake carefully on the day of consumption, as a single bowl exceeds the WHO recommended daily sodium limit of 2,000mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Medium GI (~58) but high glycemic load per bowl (~34.6) due to large rice vermicelli carbohydrate portion (~63g carbohydrate per 550g bowl), representing approximately twice the recommended single-meal carbohydrate intake for T2DM patients (~30g per meal).
type2_diabetes.guidance: Patients with Type 2 Diabetes should limit Nonya Laksa to a half-portion of noodles with full broth and toppings to reduce carbohydrate load by approximately 50%, and should prefer lower-carbohydrate Peranakan alternatives such as Assam Fish Curry or Chap Chye when dining at Nonya restaurants.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Very high saturated fat content from full-fat coconut milk (~32g saturated fat per 550g bowl, exceeding the daily recommended limit of 20g for adults) combined with high sodium (~2,310mg per bowl) and moderate cholesterol (~55mg/100g; ~303mg per bowl).
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Patients with cardiovascular disease should consume Nonya Laksa rarely (no more than once per fortnight), avoid drinking the full coconut milk broth, and balance the meal with low-saturated-fat choices for the remainder of the day, as a single bowl delivers saturated fat and sodium levels that substantially exceed cardiovascular-protective dietary targets.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Very high saturated fat from coconut milk (~32g per bowl) and high sodium are both pro-inflammatory at sustained high intake, outweighing the anti-inflammatory benefit of rempah spices and omega-3 from cockles and prawns.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Patients with rheumatoid arthritis should limit Nonya Laksa consumption and consider Assam Fish Curry as a significantly more anti-inflammatory Peranakan alternative.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Nonya Laksa (娘惹叻沙), also known as Laksa Lemak ('fat laksa' or 'rich laksa'), is the Peranakan version of Singapore's iconic laksa noodle soup — richer, creamier, and more intensely spiced than the standard Singapore hawker-centre laksa. The defining characteristic of Nonya Laksa is the use of abundant full-fat coconut milk (without dilution with evaporated milk, which is common in hawker versions) and a rempah (spice paste) made purely from traditional Nonya aromatics — lemongrass, galangal, candlenut, dried shrimp, belacan, and fresh chillies — with no curry powder, which distinguishes it from Indian-influenced laksas. The dish is served with thick rice vermicelli (bee hoon) submerged in the thick, fragrant, orange-hued coconut milk broth. Toppings typically include fresh prawns, cockles (a defining ingredient in Malacca Nonya laksa), fishcake slices, and taupok (fried tofu puffs that absorb the broth). The dish is finished with laksa leaf (daun kesum / Vietnamese coriander), which provides a distinctive peppery, citrusy aroma. Nutritionally, Nonya Laksa is one of the most calorically dense Peranakan dishes: per 100g it provides 132 kcal, 6.8g protein, 7.5g fat (5.8g saturated), and 11.5g carbohydrate. A standard 550g bowl delivers approximately 726 kcal, 37g fat (~32g saturated), 63g carbohydrate, and 2,310mg sodium. The high saturated fat and sodium make it a high-concern dish for hypertension, cardiovascular disease, and rheumatoid arthritis patients. The medium GI (~58) combined with high glycemic load (~34.6 per bowl) makes it a high-concern dish for Type 2 Diabetes management. Available at Peranakan heritage restaurants in Singapore (Candlenut, True Blue, Peramakan) and traditional Malacca laksa stalls.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values derived from IMR Malaysia MyFCD (approved primary regional source) with USDA SR Legacy cross-reference for micronutrients. No single approved source contains an exact Nonya Laksa entry; values represent a composite estimate for the complete dish as served. GI is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database rice vermicelli data. Confidence set to medium per Project Veritas v2 confidence policy (closest valid match used rather than exact dish match).
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractionation data for Nonya Laksa as a composite dish are not available in IMR Malaysia MyFCD or USDA SR Legacy as of 2026. water_g set to -1: moisture content for the complete assembled dish is not reported in available approved sources. trans_fat_g is 0.0 (confirmed negligible/zero for this traditional coconut-milk-based dish with no hydrogenated fats; confirmed per USDA SR Legacy coconut milk and seafood component data). All core macros (energy_kcal, protein_g, fat_g, carbohydrate_g) are non-zero and nutritionally plausible for a rich coconut milk noodle soup. Portions in input had multiple conflicting 'is_default: true' entries; canonical serving size of 550g used as the verified portion (broth 300g + rice noodles 100g + toppings 150g). ethnic_tag 'western' in input appears to be a data error and has been omitted. FoodEx2 code A0CKP assigned as closest defensible parent for composite noodle soup; no exact Nonya Laksa FoodEx2 entry exists. Q8 fix (iteration 3): aliases_by_language self-check performed — every string value in every language key of aliases_by_language verified to appear verbatim in the flat aliases array. Malay alias 'Laksa (Malay)' added to both aliases_by_language.malay and flat aliases to disambiguate from the English 'Laksa' entry. Tamil alias 'நோன்யா லக்சா' added explicitly as a standalone entry in both flat aliases and aliases_by_language.tamil to ensure verbatim match. Romanized aliases 'Lak-sa' and 'Niángrě Lèshā' confirmed present in flat aliases array.
